does: This paper introduces CATA, a method for continual machine unlearning in vision-language models that handles sequential removal requests over time. Rather than treating each unlearning request in isolation, CATA represents each forget request as a task vector and maintains a history of these vectors. The core innovation is a sign-aware conflict-averse aggregation mechanism that identifies and resolves conflicts between successive unlearning updates, preventing contradictory modifications to model parameters. The method aims to simultaneously achieve three objectives: effectively removing target knowledge, preserving utility of retained model capabilities, and ensuring removed knowledge does not re-emerge through subsequent updates. CATA operates within a task arithmetic framework, making it computationally efficient and practical for deployment scenarios.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in task arithmetic and parameter-efficient fine-tuning that represent model modifications as additive vectors in weight space. It extends single-shot machine unlearning research, which has focused on one-time removal of specific data or concepts, to the more realistic continual setting where multiple removal requests arrive sequentially. The paper responds to limitations in prior unlearning methods that either work only for isolated forget requests or fail to account for interference between successive updates. CATA also draws on work in continual learning and catastrophic forgetting, adapting conflict resolution strategies from that domain to the unlearning context. The approach builds upon vision-language model research that has highlighted privacy and copyright concerns in large-scale multimodal training.
enables: This work opens the door to practical deployment of continual unlearning in production vision-language systems, enabling organizations to handle real-world sequential removal requests without full model retraining. The conflict-averse aggregation framework could inspire new methods for managing parameter updates in other continual learning scenarios where competing objectives emerge over time. Future work could extend CATA to other modalities beyond vision-language models, apply it to text-only or multimodal foundation models with different architectures, and develop theoretical guarantees about knowledge removal persistence. The research also enables exploration of more sophisticated conflict resolution strategies and adaptive weighting schemes for aggregating task vectors. Additionally, this work points toward privacy-preserving deployment pipelines where models can efficiently handle user requests for data deletion or concept removal without service interruption or catastrophic performance degradation.
